skill.name: visual-reviewer
skill.description: Reviews visual outputs such as web pages, documents, and UI screens by opening previews, capturing screenshots, checking layout quality, and guiding visual fixes.
skill.summary: Screenshot-based visual review for UI, document, and layout quality checks.
skill.author: Barry-Liu-888

security_audit.summary: Static analysis over-reported weak crypto, upload, obfuscation, and Markdown command examples; those items are not confirmed as malware. However, the skill deliberately provides automatic screenshot workflows with full-screen, active-window, process-window, and region capture, plus visible-window diagnostics. This creates a high privacy risk for a community skill unless publication adds strict consent, scope, and sandbox warnings.

high_findings.title: Broad Screen Capture Workflow
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill instructs automatic screenshot review after visual output and provides scripts that can capture a full screen, active window, named process window, or arbitrary screen region. This is useful for visual QA, but it can expose unrelated windows or sensitive data if used without clear user consent and narrow capture targets.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts directly implement full-screen, active-window, process-window, and region screenshot modes. The skill text also says the workflow should trigger automatically after visual output.

high_findings.title: Window and Process Metadata Disclosure
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: When a requested window is not found, the screenshot tools enumerate visible windows and process names in debug output. This can disclose document titles, application names, or other workspace context to logs or model context.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both implementations explicitly collect visible window titles and process names for diagnostics. The behavior is local, but the privacy exposure is clear.

medium_findings.title: Native Window API Access
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The screenshot tools call Win32 APIs through ctypes and PowerShell Add-Type to activate windows, inspect window geometry, and enumerate visible windows. This is legitimate for screenshots, but it expands host interaction beyond plain file inspection.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The native API bindings are explicit and used by the screenshot workflow. I did not find evidence of malicious persistence or exfiltration, so this is a controlled capability risk rather than confirmed malware.

low_findings.title: Shell Command Instructions Are Operational Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for command-injection intent: The many static external-command hits in SKILL.md and the checklist are Markdown examples showing how to run the bundled screenshot script or CSS snippets. They do not concatenate untrusted input into a shell inside the skill files, but they still document commands an agent may execute.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backtick content is displayed as documentation examples and table text. I found no direct dynamic shell execution in those Markdown sections.

low_findings.title: Static Labels for Weak Crypto, Upload, and Obfuscation Are Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The weak-cryptography hits point to prose, comments, or argparse text rather than cryptographic code. The screen-capture-upload label points to coordinate calculation, and the high-entropy labels are explained by Chinese text and Markdown structure. No evidence found of upload endpoints, encoded payloads, or encrypted blobs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found normal screenshot code and documentation at the flagged locations. I did not find network upload logic or cryptographic operations.

low_findings.title: Localhost Server Guidance
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for external network exfiltration: The hardcoded URL is a localhost preview URL used to view generated HTML. It does not send data to a remote service, but it may expose local preview content to the local machine while the server runs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is explicitly http://localhost with a local file path pattern. I found no remote host, token, webhook, or upload call.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Screen Capture Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill instructs automatic screenshot review after visual output and provides scripts that can capture a full screen, active window, named process window, or arbitrary screen region. This is useful for visual QA, but it can expose unrelated windows or sensitive data if used without clear user consent and narrow capture targets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The scripts directly implement full-screen, active-window, process-window, and region screenshot modes. The skill text also says the workflow should trigger automatically after visual output.

dangerous_patterns.title: Window and Process Metadata Disclosure
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: When a requested window is not found, the screenshot tools enumerate visible windows and process names in debug output. This can disclose document titles, application names, or other workspace context to logs or model context.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Both implementations explicitly collect visible window titles and process names for diagnostics. The behavior is local, but the privacy exposure is clear.

dangerous_patterns.title: Native Window API Access
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The screenshot tools call Win32 APIs through ctypes and PowerShell Add-Type to activate windows, inspect window geometry, and enumerate visible windows. This is legitimate for screenshots, but it expands host interaction beyond plain file inspection.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The native API bindings are explicit and used by the screenshot workflow. I did not find evidence of malicious persistence or exfiltration, so this is a controlled capability risk rather than confirmed malware.

content.value_statement: Visual defects often hide until a real preview is inspected. This skill captures rendered output and guides layout, spacing, typography, color, and interaction fixes.
actual_capabilities: Guides screenshot capture for web pages, documents, presentations, windows, and screen regions.
Reviews layout, spacing, typography, color contrast, interaction states, data displays, and edge cases.
Provides a scoring checklist for visual quality dimensions.
Suggests root causes and fixes for visual defects in generated outputs.
Supports repeated screenshot validation after visual fixes.
limitations: Screenshot scripts are Windows-oriented and depend on local screen access.
The skill does not include automated accessibility measurement tools.
Full-screen or active-window capture may include unrelated sensitive content.
It cannot verify backend behavior beyond what is visible in the rendered output.

use_cases.title: Review a generated web page
use_cases.description: Inspect a rendered HTML page for overlap, spacing problems, text overflow, and weak color contrast.

use_cases.title: Check a product mockup document
use_cases.description: Open a document or presentation and review typography, alignment, tables, and visual hierarchy.

use_cases.title: Validate AI-generated UI changes
use_cases.description: Capture before and after screens, confirm fixes, and produce a concise visual review report.

prompt_templates.title: Basic visual check
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of one local page or document.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the visual output I just created. Capture the preview and report layout, spacing, typography, and color issues.

prompt_templates.title: Fix visible defects
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the rendered interface, identify visible defects, fix the clear CSS or layout issues, and verify the result with another screenshot.

prompt_templates.title: Responsive review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this web UI at mobile, tablet, and desktop sizes. Focus on overlap, text overflow, table behavior, and touch target size.

prompt_templates.title: Release visual audit
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a visual audit for this release screen. Capture evidence, rank issues by severity, apply safe fixes, and summarize remaining risks.

output_examples.input: A dashboard page was generated and previewed in a browser.
output: Found three issues: a clipped chart label, inconsistent card padding, and low contrast helper text.
Fixed the card padding and helper text color. The chart label needs a wider legend area.
Rechecked the preview and confirmed no new overlap at desktop width.

output_examples.input: A presentation slide deck needs a visual quality pass.
output: The title hierarchy is clear, but two tables use narrow columns that wrap key labels.
Recommended widening the first column and reducing secondary text weight.
No critical color contrast issues were visible in the captured slide.

output_examples.input: A mobile layout has been updated after a CSS change.
output: At 320px width, the navigation and primary button remain usable.
A long status string still overflows the summary row.
Suggested adding wrapping or truncation for unbroken strings.

best_practices: Capture the smallest window or region that contains the output under review.
Close or hide unrelated sensitive windows before using screenshot modes.
Re-run the visual review after each layout fix to confirm the rendered result.
anti_patterns: Do not use full-screen capture when a targeted window or region is enough.
Do not rely only on source code inspection for visual quality decisions.
Do not make broad style rewrites when a small layout fix resolves the issue.

faq.question: What does this skill review?
faq.answer: It reviews rendered visual outputs, including web pages, documents, presentations, and user interface screens.

faq.question: Does it automatically fix issues?
faq.answer: It can guide direct fixes for clear visual defects, then verify the result with another screenshot.

faq.question: Does it need screenshot access?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow depends on local screenshots, so users should limit capture scope and protect sensitive windows.

faq.question: Can it check accessibility?
faq.answer: It can visually review contrast, focus states, and touch targets, but it is not a full accessibility scanner.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Why is publication risk high?
faq.answer: The bundled scripts can capture broad screen areas and expose window metadata, which creates privacy risk in community distribution.
